titleStmt.title: Letter from Paul Biggane to the Under Secretary of Ireland, 3 May 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Paul Biggane

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from Paul Biggane (1885-1937) to the under secretary of Ireland regarding Biggane's application for civil employment. While on furlough from the Indian Police Force Biggane is anxious to secure some form of work and details his experience within the crime sector and includes that he has knowledge of Italian, German and French.At the time this letter was written Sir Matthew Nathan (1862-1939) was serving as the under secretary for Ireland. Nathan took up the position in 1914 and remained until his resignation in 1916 following the outbreak of the Easter Rising.

msIdentifier.collection: Chief Secretary's Office Registered Papers, CSO RP/1916/7560

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Sunday's Well Cork 3 May 1916 Dear Sir, I recently arrived
home on 19 months furlough
from India where I have
served for 10 1/2 years in
the Indian Police. As I am not
permitted to enter into
military obligations I am
anxious to secure temporary
employment of a civil
nature. I have been in
charge of districts in
India and for 19
months before taking
leave I was on
special Duty in the
Secret (Political) Branch
of the Criminal Investigation
Department, United Provinces.
For 8 months I officiated
as Asst. to D. Inspr
Genl in charge of the
Secret Branch. I have
thus had considerable
experience of both
political and ordinary
crime. When I heard of
the outbreak in Dublin
I wired to the India
office suggesting the
possibility of employment
at Dublin. I have
been advised by the
Officer in charge, Information
Bureau, to apply to you.
I also offered my
services to the local
police authorities but
was told that they
were not needed. I would, subject
to the control of the
India Office, place no
restrictions on this offer,
of my services. I am
willing to undertake other
office on outdoor work.
I am 30 years of age
and free from political
or religious prejudices.
A possible qualification
is an ability to read
ordinary material in
French, German and Italian. I have tried my
best to obtain permission
to apply for a commission
and have failed. I
hope that you will
receive this application
formally and that my
services will be utilised
if it is at all possible Yours faithfully P.Biggan The Under Secretary of State for Ireland Dublin Castle
